coredata.dc:title: Ni–Co–P functionalized Nitrogen-Doped-Carbon quantum dots for efficient methanol electrooxidation and nanofluid applications
coredata.dc:creator: Saeed, Ebtisam
Abdelwahab, Ibrahim
Abdelwahab, Abdalla
coredata.dc:description: Nanocomposite materials that can be utilized for different applications related to energy production and energy transfer are in great demand. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) are prepared via a facile microwave-assisted method. The as-prepared N-CDs are further functionalized with nickel cobaltite (NiCo2O4/N-CDs), nickel cobalt tungstate (Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs) and nickel cobalt phosphide (NiCoP/N-CDs) through a hydrothermal process. These nanocomposites can function as anode electro-catalysts for direct methanol fuel cells, or as nanoparticles for the preparation of nanofluids. NiCoP/N-CDs exhibits the highest performance in both applications. It achieves a peak current density of 74.7 mA cm−2 for methanol electrooxidation in an alkaline medium with current retention of 92 % after 100 charging cycles. The high catalytic activity of NiCoP/N-CDs is attributed to its high crystallinity, large pore size, and nanoflakes-like surface morphology. The high thermal stability and electrical conductivity of the nanocomposite-based nanofluids hold promise for various heat transfer operations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposites
Carbon Quantum Dots
Methanol electrooxidation
Catalysis
Nanofluids
Nanoflakes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs)
Preparation of NiCo2O4/N-CDs, Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs and NiCoP/N-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by Science, Technology & Innovation Funding Authority (STDF), Egypt (Grant No Youth-39390).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of the synthesized samples.

FESEM images of (a) N-CDs, (b) NiCo2O4/N-CDs, (c) Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs and (d) NiCoP/N-CDs. HRTEM images of (e) NiCo2O4/N-CDs, (f) Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs, (g) NiCoP/N-CDs. (h) EDS spectrum and elemental quantitative data of NiCoP/N-CDs.
Cyclic voltammograms of (a) NiCo2O4/N-CDs, (b) Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs and (c) NiCoP/N-CDs in 1 M KOH at different scan rates.

Cyclic voltammograms of (a) NiCo2O4/N-CDs, (b) Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs and (c) NiCoP/N-CDs in 1 M KOH + 1 M MeOH at different scan rates. Inset is a plot of the anodic current density versus the square root of the scan rate for NiCoP/N-CDs. (d) Cyclic voltammograms of NiCoP/N-CDs in both 1 M KOH and 1 M KOH + 1 M MeOH electrolytes at 60 mV s−1.

Cyclic voltammograms of (a) NiCo2O4/N-CDs, (b) Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs and (c) NiCoP/N-CDs in different methanol concentrations at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. (d) Cyclic voltammograms for NiCoP/N-CDs after 1, 50 and 100 charging cycles.
(a) The conductivity of the nanofluids as a function of temperature. (b) The average hydrodynamic particle size of the nanofluids.
XRD and surface area analysis data.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *Surface area analysis for N-CDs could not be realized due to thermal instability during the degassing process.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Zeta-potential, hydrodynamic diameter, and conductivity values of the nanofluids.

head.ce:title: Ni–Co–P functionalized Nitrogen-Doped-Carbon quantum dots for efficient methanol electrooxidation and nanofluid applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science and Nanotechnology Department, Faculty of Postgraduate Studies for Advanced Sciences, Beni-Suef University, Beni-Suef 62511, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Postgraduate Studies for Advanced Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Materials Science and Nanotechnology Department, Faculty of Postgraduate Studies for Advanced Sciences, Beni-Suef University, Beni-Suef 62511, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National University of Singapore, 3 Science Drive 3, Singapore, 117543, Singapore
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National University of Singapore, 3 Science Drive 3, Singapore, 117543, Singapore

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Science, Galala University, Sokhna, Suez 43511, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Science, Galala University, Sokhna, Suez 43511, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Science, Galala University, Sokhna, Suez 43511, Egypt.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanocomposite materials that can be utilized for different applications related to energy production and energy transfer are in great demand. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) are prepared via a facile microwave-assisted method. The as-prepared N-CDs are further functionalized with nickel cobaltite (NiCo2O4/N-CDs), nickel cobalt tungstate (Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs) and nickel cobalt phosphide (NiCoP/N-CDs) through a hydrothermal process. These nanocomposites can function as anode electro-catalysts for direct methanol fuel cells, or as nanoparticles for the preparation of nanofluids. NiCoP/N-CDs exhibits the highest performance in both applications. It achieves a peak current density of 74.7 mA cm−2 for methanol electrooxidation in an alkaline medium with current retention of 92 % after 100 charging cycles. The high catalytic activity of NiCoP/N-CDs is attributed to its high crystallinity, large pore size, and nanoflakes-like surface morphology. The high thermal stability and electrical conductivity of the nanocomposite-based nanofluids hold promise for various heat transfer operations.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, alternative fuels and energy sources have become increasingly significant due to the combustion pollutants associated with fossil fuels. These reliable energy sources also reduce energy security concerns related to volatile fossil fuel supplies. Methanol is an eco-friendly alternative to fossil fuels. Currently, direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) hold great potential for applications such as electric vehicles and portable electronics due to their low operation temperature, low pollution emissions, and high energy density [1,2]. DMFCs are at an advantage over other alcohol (e.g., ethanol)-based fuel cells, since the methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) does not require the breakage of strong C C bonds leading to rapid reaction kinetics [2,3]. In DMFCs, the anodic MOR is critical in determining the fuel cell efficiency [4].

Platinum (Pt)-based electro-catalysts and their alloys have been considered the most efficient catalysts for MOR [1,4–6]. As an example, Zhang et. al. [4] have recently reported the preparation of a Pt/RuO2 single-atom catalyst via a simple impregnation–adsorption method. Their catalyst showed good electro-catalytic activity towards methanol oxidation with a current density of 6766 mA mg−1 Pt. However, due to the high cost and scarcity of noble metals (e.g., Pt), noble-metal-free catalysts have attracted great attention in catalysis. Over the past few years, various non-noble transition metals such as Ni, Co, Fe, and their compounds have been utilized as fuel cell catalysts due to their low cost, high availability, high efficiency, and good thermal and electrical conductivities. For the MOR, Ni and Co-based catalysts are quite promising owing to their good surface oxidation properties and high abundance. In this regard, several approaches were developed to synthesize binary and ternary Ni–Co oxides that have high electrical conductivity and enhanced activity for the MOR. One of these Ni–Co catalytic compounds is nickel cobaltite (NiCo2O4) which can be obtained from NiO and Co3O4 precursors [7]. The Ni–Co oxides can be embedded inside a carbon matrix for better dispersibility and protection against leaching. Ko et al. reported the preparation of core–shell-like NiCo2O4 nanoparticles decorated on multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) using a dry synthesis technique [8]. The NiCo2O4/MWCNTs nanocomposite demonstrated good stability and catalytic activity for methanol electro-oxidation. Doping the Ni–Co based catalyst with another element such as phosphorous (P) was found to modify its electronic structure and morphology and boost its electrochemical activity [9,10]. Luminescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) represent a new type of catalyst support materials that have attracted tremendous interest in catalysis, photovoltaics, and biological applications [11–15]. The appeal of CDs stems from their large surface-to-volume ratio, low cytotoxicity, environmentally friendly preparation methods, and facile modification. CDs can be doped with nitrogen (N-CDs) in order to increase their surface area and photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) [11].

In addition, the nano-sized CDs-supported Ni–Co compounds can be added to conventional heat transfer fluids such as water [16], ethylene glycol (EG) [17], mineral oil [18], or a mixture of these fluids [19], to enhance their thermophysical properties. These thermophysical properties include thermal conductivity, specific heat, density, and dynamic viscosity [20]. Nanofluids (i.e., the nanoparticles/fluid suspension) have been widely used to efficiently transfer heat from one medium to another, reducing wasteful energy consumption for heating or cooling operations. They have found applications in many areas such as heat exchangers, solar thermal collectors, electronic cooling, and automotive radiators [21]. Metal oxides (e.g., Al2O3, TiO2, ZnO, CuO, MgO, NiCo2O4, etc.) and carbon-based nanomaterials (e.g., graphene, diamond, MWCNT, CDs, etc.) are preferred nanoparticles due to their high thermal conductivity which increases the effective thermal conductivity of the nanofluid. Furthermore, the percolation, Brownian motion, and micro-level convection of the nanoparticles decrease the thermal boundary layer between the working fluid and its contact surfaces, and thus, enhance the efficiency of the energy conversion [22]. Other material properties such as particle size and particle shape can also have a large impact on the effective thermal conductivity of the nanofluid [23,24]. For example, Jeong et al. studied the effect of the nanoparticle shape on the thermal conductivity of zinc oxide (ZnO)-based nanofluids [24]. It was found that the rectangular-shaped ZnO nanoparticles have higher dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity than those of a spherical shape.

In this work, dual-functional nanocomposites made of N-CDs functionalized with non-noble transition metal oxides and phosphides were prepared and tested for both MOR and nanofluid applications. N-CDs were first prepared by a rapid microwave-assisted method using citric acid and urea as precursors. The resultant N-CDs were further functionalized with NiCo2O4 by a hydrothermal process. The NiCo2O4/N-CDs nanocomposites were tested as anode electro-catalysts for MOR in an alkaline medium of 1 M potassium hydroxide (KOH) + 1 M methanol (MeOH). Furthermore, the thermophysical properties (including thermal stability, hydrodynamic size, and Zeta potential) of NiCo2O4/N-CDs nanoparticles-based EG nanofluids were investigated. The effect of adding another metal (i.e., tungsten (W)) to form nickel cobalt tungstate Ni0.5Co0.5WO4 or non-metal (i.e., P) to form nickel cobalt phosphide NiCoP) on the electrochemical activity and nanofluid properties was also studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs were synthesized by a facile microwave-assisted method utilizing citric acid and urea as the precursors for carbon and nitrogen, respectively. A mixture of 2 g of citric acid and 1 g of urea dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water was prepared. The solution was then transferred into a domestic microwave and irradiated with a power of 450 W for 10 min. A black powder was obtained, diluted with 100 mL deionized water, and filtrated with a dialysis bag to remove large particles [25]. The color of the final N-CDs solution was brownish yellow.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NiCo2O4/N-CDs, Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs and NiCoP/N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For NiCo2O4/N-CDs, 0.250 g of nickel acetate and 0.5 g of cobalt acetate were dissolved in 100 mL of the N-CDs solution in the presence of 0.250 g of tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB) to prevent particle agglomeration. The solution was sonicated in an ultrasonic probe sonicator for 10 min, transferred into stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Thereafter, the obtained powder was washed, dried at 60 °C for 10 h, and finally calcined in a vacuum drier at 360 °C for 3 h.
For Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs and NiCoP/N-CDs, the same preparation procedure was followed except for the addition of 1 mmole of sodium tungstate or black phosphorous to the Ni and Co precursors.

The crystallinity and phase identification of the nanocomposites were determined by powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Empyrean XRD Diffractometer, Malvern Panalytical, the Netherlands) using CuKα radiation with an accelerating voltage of 40 kV. The mean particle size of the nanoparticles in the different samples was estimated using Scherrer’s equation. The surface area and pore structure were investigated with N2 adsorption/desorption at 77 K using a surface area analyzer (TriStar II 3020, Micromeritics, USA). Prior to the N2-adsorption/desorption analysis, the samples were outgassed at 110 °C for 3 h under He gas flow. The morphology of the nanocomposites was characterized using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (EVO MA10 Carl Zeiss Microscopy GmbH, Germany) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEM2100, Jeol, Japan). The elemental mapping analysis was performed using energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) (X-MAX 80, Oxford Instruments, the UK). The electrochemical performance of the electrocatalysts for methanol electro-oxidation was evaluated using an electrochemical workstation (Autolab PGSTAT302N Potentiostat/Galvanostat, Metrohm, the Netherlands). Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed with a heating rate of 10 °C/minute using LABSYS evo DTA/DSC sensor (Setaram Instrumentation, France). The Zeta potential and the hydrodynamic particle size of the nanofluids were determined using Zetasizer Nano S90/ZS90 (Malvern Panalytical, the Netherlands). The conductivity of the nanofluids was measured with Jenway 4520 Bench Conductivity Meter (Cole-Parmer Instrument, the USA). The conductivity probe was inserted into a small vessel filled with the nanofluid. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed to evaluate the Zeta- potential and the hydrodynamic particle size of the suspended nanoparticles in EG.

5 mg of the as-prepared materials were dispersed in 400 μL isopropanol and 15 μL Nafion solution 5 % and sonicated for 10 min. The working electrode was prepared by depositing 10 μL of the suspended ink onto the active area of a glassy carbon electrode (3 mm in diameter) and then dried under an infrared lamp.

The electrochemical performance of the nanocomposites as electro-catalysts for methanol electro-oxidation was tested in a three-electrode electrochemical cell setup in which platinum (Pt) sheet and silver/silver chloride (Ag/AgCl) electrodes acted as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The glassy carbon electrode served as the working electrode. The electrochemical measurements of the electro-catalysts were tested in 1 M KOH solution and 1 M KOH + 1 M methanol (MeOH) electrolyte through cyclic voltammetry measurements at different scan rates from 10 − 100 mV‧s−1.

The nanofluids were prepared by mixing 3 mg of the nanocomposites with 6 mg of sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate surfactant and then adding the mixture to EG as a base fluid in a weight concentration of 0.5 wt% followed by stirring for 30 min.

Fig. 1b shows the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of the synthesized materials. The isotherms are of type IV with an open H4 hysteresis loop within a relative pressure range between 0.6 and 0.9, except for Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs which has a closed loop. The specific surface area (SBET) of the nanocomposites was determined by the standard BET (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller) equation, while the pore size distribution and pore volume were obtained from the desorption branch of the isotherms by applying the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) model. The textural parameters of the different nanocomposites are listed in Table 1. The data reveals that NiCo2O4/N-CDs and NiCoP/N-CDs possess well-developed mesoporous structures with hierarchical pores which are highly desirable for use in catalysis. High SBET of 63.1 m2/g, pore volume of 0.158 cm3 g−1 and pore size of 5.7 nm are noticed for NiCo2O4/N-CDs. In contrast, the Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs nanocomposite showed a more compact structure with SBET of 4.3 m2/g.

To gain insight into the microstructure of the prepared nanocomposites, we performed FESEM (Fig. 2 a-d) and HRTEM (Fig. 2e-g) characterizations. The morphology of N-CDs is shown in Fig. 2a in which homogenous distribution of carbon nanoparticles is observed. The N-CDs underwent a significant morphological change after the functionalization treatment of transition metal oxides and phosphides. In Fig. 2b, a morphology of ultra-small, interconnected nanoparticles is acquired for NiCo2O4/N-CDs which explains its high surface area data. On the other hand, aggregation of closely stacked nanoparticles is observed for Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs (Fig. 2c). Novel morphology of 3D crinkled porous architectures is attained for NiCoP/N-CDs (Fig. 2d). The architectures are coupled with spherical interconnected NiCoP nanoparticles as shown in the insets of Fig. 2d. It is worth mentioning that several morphologies (i.e., nanoflakes, nanoplates, nanospheres or irregular shapes) were reported for nickel cobaltite depending on the temperature, duration and the type of surfactant used in the hydrothermal process [30,31]. The interior surface morphologies of the nanocomposites are investigated by HRTEM. The HRTEM analysis reveals that the composite nanoparticles are homogenously distributed within the carbon matrix. The particle size range of NiCo2O4/N-CDs aggregation (Fig. 2e) is from 6.89 − 9.35 nm, while larger particle sizes of 8.64–44.92 nm are manifested in the Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs aggregation (Fig. 2f). The NiCoP/N-CDs sample (Fig. 2g) exhibits nanoflakes-like surface morphology which is in line with previous observations [32]. The chemical composition and homogeneity of NiCoP/N-CDs were confirmed with EDS spot analysis of different grains. Fig. 2h shows an integrated EDS spectrum of NiCoP/N-CDs together with tabulated weight and atomic percentages of the constituent elements. The atomic ratio between Ni:Co:P is about 1:2:2, equivalent to 1:1:1 M composition ratio, which is in agreement with XRD data [33]. Moreover, no contaminated elements were detected by EDS indicating the purity and homogeneity of the preparation process.

To explore the electro-catalytic activity of the nanocomposites towards methanol electro-oxidation, electrochemical measurements were carried out using a three-electrode system as described in the experimental procedures section. Fig. 3 shows the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) for NiCo2O4/N-CDs, Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs and NiCoP/N-CDs in 1 M KOH at different scan rates. The maximum potential window for Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs is between −0.2–0.5 V, while for NiCo2O4/N-CDs and NiCoP/N-CDs it is between 0 and 0.6 V. Indicating that NiCo2O4/N-CDs and NiCoP/N-CDs are stable electrodes at high positive potential than Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs. An oxide formation peak appeared above the potential of 0.4 V. For all samples, the recorded current densities are affected by changing the scan rates, indicating the process is depending on the ions diffusion inside the micro/mesopores of the nanocomposites and the oxidation process is kinetically controlled. The current density for NiCoP/N-CDs is 25-fold as high as that for Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs.

Although the current densities for NiCo2O4/N-CDs and NiCoP/N-CDs are roughly the same in 1 M KOH, a big difference is noticed in the 1 M KOH + 1 M MeOH electrolyte (Fig. 4 ). A maximum anodic current density of 29.1 mA cm−2 is attained for NiCo2O4/N-CDs at a scan rate of 60 mV s−1 (Fig. 4a). The methanol oxidation activity on Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs is vanishingly low as only 2.0 mA cm−2 current density is delivered at a scan rate of 60 mV s−1 (Fig. 4b). The highest current density is registered for NiCoP/N-CDs with 74.7 mA cm−2 at 60 mV s−1 (Fig. 4c). The high electrochemical activity of NiCoP/N-CDs can be attributed to its nanoflakes-like morphology that provides more catalytic active sites for methanol and to the synergistic effect between Ni, Co and P. The inset of Fig. 4c, shows the linear dependence of the current density on the square root of the scan rate. Fig. 4d compares between the catalytic activities of NiCoP/N-CDs in the presence and absence of methanol. The current density increased from 24.9 to 74.7 mA cm−2 in presence of methanol, indicating that NiCoP/N-CDs is a promising electro-catalyst for methanol oxidation.

Fig. 5 depicts the electrochemical performance of the nanocomposites in different methanol concentrations. The anodic current did not increase with increasing methanol concentration for NiCo2O4/N-CDs (Fig. 5a). This observation suggests the saturation of the active sites on the surface of the electrode. A gradual increase in the current density is observed for Ni0.5Co0.5WO4 (Fig. 5b) and NiCoP/N-CDs (Fig. 5c) with increasing methanol concentration. This implies that the ternary compounds have more active sites into which extra methanol molecules can penetrate at higher concentrations. Moreover, the long-term catalytic stability of the nanocomposites is an important factor that has to be evaluated, as it determines whether the electrode material satisfies the long-term performance requirements for real power applications. The stability of NiCoP/N-CDs electrocatalyst was evaluated by performing cyclic voltammetry measurements for 100 discharge/charge cycles at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 in 1 M KOH + 1 M MeOH (Fig. 5d). A current density retention of 92 % was maintained after 100 cycles, attesting to the long-term stability of NiCoP/N-CDs. The reduction in the current density can be ascribed to the methanol consumption during the oxidation process [7].

TGA was performed on NiCoP/N-CDs to evaluate its thermal stability over time as the temperature changes (Fig. 6 ). TGA thermogram showed a gradual weight loss of up to 12.2 % between 25 and 400 °C, which can be attributed to the desorption of water molecules and the removal of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the nanocomposite. No significant weight loss was observed up to 700 °C, indicating the good thermal stability of the nanocomposite [34].

The electrical conductivity results obtained for the four nanofluids at 20 °C are listed in Table 2 . NiCoP/N-CDs exhibits the highest conductivity (62.9 μS), implying low surface resistance between the suspended NiCoP/N-CDs nanoparticles. Fig. 7 a illustrates the conductivity of nanofluids as a function of temperature. A gradual increase in conductivity is realized with increasing temperature. It can be interpreted that as the nanofluid temperature increases, its viscosity decreases which makes the nanoparticles diffuse faster (Brownian motion) in the base fluid and in turn increases its conductivity. The shape of the suspended nanoparticles can have a significant impact on the conductivity of the nanofluid [35]. In this context, the conductivity enhancement of NiCoP/N-CDs can be partially attributed to its morphology and high diffusion rate.

DLS utilizes the time variation in the intensity of scattered light from particles in a solution under laser illumination to determine the hydrodynamic size distribution of the suspended nanoparticles [36]. For non-spherical particles, DLS will give the diameter of a sphere that same average translational diffusion coefficient as the particle being measured (that is the hydrodynamic diameter). Therefore, the hydrodynamic diameter will depend not only on the size of the particle but also on any surface structure as different shapes will experience different hydrodynamic forces (including hydrodynamic hindering, electrostatic repulsion and long-range van der Waals attraction) [36,37]. The hydrodynamic diameter of the prepared nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 7a. The average hydrodynamic diameter of N-CDs is 141 nm. NiCoP/N-CDs has the lowest average hydrodynamic diameter of about 317 nm among the different nanocomposite (Table 2). The smaller the hydrodynamic diameter is, the more rapid the Brownian motion of the particle gets to be and the more conductive the nanofluid becomes. Similar to the hydrodynamic size, the value of the Zeta-potential is related to the particle morphology [38]. Zeta potential is a measure of the effective electric charge on the nanoparticle’s surface and its magnitude provides information about particle stability. The Zeta-potential values for the four nanofluids are represented in Table 2. NiCoP/N-CDs exhibits a Zeta-potential of − 4.1 mV, while N-CDs, NiCo2O4/N-CDs and Ni0.5Co0.5WO4/N-CDs have the values of − 36.4, −25.5 and − 13.2 mV, respectively. Nanoparticles with a Zeta-potential between − 10 and + 10 mV (e.g., NiCoP/N-CDs) are considered neutral nanoparticles with high salt stability, while those with Zeta-potential values of less than − 30 mV are considered strongly anionic [37]. Although NiCoP/N-CDs are neutral nanoparticles in EG, they are sterically protected against agglomeration by the TBAB ligands.

In summary, a facile and rapid microwave process was employed for the preparation of N-CDs. The as-prepared N-CDs were further functionalized with NiCo2O4, Ni0.5Co0.5WO4, or NiCoP. These nanocomposite materials benefit from dual functionality; anode electrocatalyst for MOR and suspended particles for nanofluid applications. The electrochemical activity and nanofluid properties of the nanocomposite depend on its morphology. NiCoP/N-CDs exhibits a well-developed nanoflake-like morphology which results in enhanced diffusivity and adsorption of methanol. A current density of 74.7 mA cm−2 was attained for NiCoP/N-CDs with retention reaching 92 % after 100 repetitive cycles. In addition, NiCoP/N-CDs possesses high thermal stability and electrical conductivity making it a promising material for energy and nanofluid applications.
This research was funded by Science, Technology & Innovation Funding Authority (STDF), Egypt (Grant No Youth-39390).

Ebtisam Saeed: Methodology, Investigation. Ibrahim Abdelwahab: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Abdalla Abdelwahab: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Visualization, Project administration, Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ self-assembled N-rich carbon on pristine graphene as a highly effective support and cocatalyst of short Pt nanoparticle chains for superior electrocatalytic activity toward methanol oxidation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Fan, X., et al., In situ self-assembled N-rich carbon on pristine graphene as a highly effective support and cocatalyst of short Pt nanoparticle chains for superior electrocatalytic activity toward methanol oxidation. Nanoscale, 2021. 13(43): p. 18332-18339.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-Based Electrocatalysts for Methanol Electro-Oxidation: Progress, Opportunities, and Challenges

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tong, Y., et al., Metal-Based Electrocatalysts for Methanol Electro-Oxidation: Progress, Opportunities, and Challenges. Small, 2021. 17(9): p. 1904126.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A comprehensive review on recent material development of passive direct methanol fuel cell

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Munjewar, S.S., S.B. Thombre, and R.K. Mallick, A comprehensive review on recent material development of passive direct methanol fuel cell. Ionics, 2017. 23(1): p. 1-18.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Single-atom catalyst for high-performance methanol oxidation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, Z., et al., Single-atom catalyst for high-performance methanol oxidation. Nature Communications, 2021. 12(1): p. 5235.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Local electrocatalytic activity of PtRu supported on nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes towards methanol oxidation by scanning electrochemical microscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gupta, D., et al., Local electrocatalytic activity of PtRu supported on nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes towards methanol oxidation by scanning electrochemical microscopy. Journal of Materials Chemistry A, 2021. 9(37): p. 21291-21301.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-precious Sn as alternative substitute metal in graphene-based catalysts for methanol electrooxidation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hasa, B., et al., Non-precious Sn as alternative substitute metal in graphene-based catalysts for methanol electrooxidation. Journal of Applied Electrochemistry, 2022. 52(3): p. 509-520.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of pore geometry on the electrocatalytic performance of nickel cobaltite/ carbon xerogel nanocomposite for methanol oxidation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: El-Deeb, M.M., et al., Effect of pore geometry on the electrocatalytic performance of nickel cobaltite/ carbon xerogel nanocomposite for methanol oxidation. Electrochimica Acta, 2018. 259: p. 77-85.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile Synthesis of Core/Shell-like NiCo2O4-Decorated MWCNTs and its Excellent Electrocatalytic Activity for Methanol Oxidation

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Three-Dimensional Hierarchical Nickel Cobalt Phosphide Nanoflowers as an Efficient Electrocatalyst for the Hydrogen Evolution Reaction under Both Acidic and Alkaline Conditions
